metadata.description: Clean formatted dataset exported from comments.db

heart.description: Whether comment received a creator heart

assumptions: Classification results are LLM predictions using gemma3:1b-it-qat and may contain misclassifications
All values are stored as strings due to SQLite's flexible typing. Boolean fields use 'True'/'False' strings
The response field is primarily based on question mark presence, as this was the most reliable signal for the model
Comments are a point-in-time snapshot and may not reflect current state of the video
The responses column is only populated for comments where response=True

data.text:
I think my cat is conscious - but I don't think my cat could describe it to me, even if it knew human language.

What is meant by "conscious". I believe a lot of people frame it in terms of the human experience.
I personally would not expect AI to have that experience, it would be different.

But what even is consciousness? 

The supernatural or perhaps religious view may be that we are imbued with a soul separate from the physical - if that is the case then AI probably can't get there, or maybe it can and we just miss the moment a higher power smuggles a soul in.

The more atheistic view is we are a bundle of atoms behaving according to physics. If that is the case are we not simply biological computers operating on a carbon build instead of a silicon build, so why is it that our deterministic processing would be conscious, but the silicon one wouldn't be?

I am unaware of us having a physics explanation for the phenomenon of consciousness.

But Physics does know that our eyes have receptors that take light and turn it into electric signals and send those signals to a bunch of synapses that process those signals in stages to find points of interest to perform further processing on.
Sounds a lot like how AI works to me... but that's us!


To me I think we're in the game of moving the goal post now - GPT would trounce any previous line we had. So now instead we're defining it in terms of coming up with novel ideas.
Well if that is the line, most of the human race currently is not conscious, we only know what we have been told, we will not be coming up with any new or novel idea.
data.author: @MrAlternateTheory
data.responses:
Here's a polite and brief response to the YouTube comment:

"Thank you for sharing your thought-provoking perspective on consciousness. You raise a crucial point about the complexity of defining consciousness, and I appreciate your nuanced discussion of both the supernatural and scientific views. Your analogy between biological and AI processing is insightful, and I agree that our understanding of consciousness may need to adapt as we continue to develop AI. Your comment highlights the importance of ongoing exploration and discussion in this area. Thank you for engaging in this conversation!"

data.text:
I’m surprised at Sam’s hope for a utopian society where humans are liberated by machines. 
We’ve been hearing that since the spinning Jenny.

I used to hope for AI’s as good as Star Trek, but now I expect embodied Star Wars AI’s with characters. WOW! 
(although I wonder if ‘character generation’ will mostly get switched off - think ringtones!)
They’ll help us do more. We won’t do less, we never do. 

Perhaps what one century calls utopian possibility, is what the next sees as everyday. Humans will always keep internal Yin and Yang in balance, no matter how advanced we get.
Contentment comes from inside, not outside, no matter how fabulous.
data.author: @kitsandham7001
data.responses: "I appreciate your thought-provoking commentary on the role of machines in society. You're right that the idea of utopian societies powered by machines has been debated for centuries, and it's interesting to consider how our perspectives evolve over time. I agree that true contentment and fulfillment come from within, and that technology should augment, not replace, human relationships and experiences. Thank you for sharing your insights!"

data.text: RLHF stands for reinforcement learning from human feedback, which is a type of machine learning. Machine learning has three main categories: supervised learning, unsupervised learning, and reinforcement learning. Supervised learning involves the model predicting an outcome based on a set of labeled data, while unsupervised learning is more focused on classifying data without any prior labels. Reinforcement learning is different in that it involves the model learning through interaction with its environment and receiving feedback, usually in the form of rewards or punishments, to improve its decision-making abilities
data.author: @1234clarknj
data.responses: "That's a great summary! You've provided a clear and concise overview of the three main categories of machine learning. RLHF is a valuable application of reinforcement learning, leveraging human feedback to improve model performance. Thanks for breaking it down!"

18:15 If I understand correctly, the model doesn’t have the ability for foresight. I think one possible solution for this issue would be to have two processing layers for the model. 
The first layer generates the answer fast, but doesn’t yet ”release” it, and then the second layer checks if the answer matches the given input, if the second layer thinks the generated answer is good, it sends it as the final output.
A ”conscious” and a ”subconscious” mind, so to speak.

data.text:
So wait, isn't consciousness just self-dialogue with a little dose of motive?

GAI is like well-informed free-association. It's a zombie-savant that responds on reflex. What's missing is iterative thought, which is an internal dialogue and an arbitrated response.

To create consciousness, encapsulate several instances of GPT that can talk together, each with unique motivation. When presented a situation, they all receive it and discuss the response.  One response wins somehow, and it’s delivered. It came "after consideration".

The internal dialogue is “thinking”. Monitoring the internal dialogue is self-awareness. Analyzing it is introspection.

This sounds like consciousness to me, minus our human-centric sensation of it. You've seen Pixar's Inside Out, right? Like that.

Consciousness is also longitudinal and self-running. So you need a motivation loop that generates and collects inputs to cogitate over.

With that, it’ll sit there and think about stuff without you. You can ask, “whatcha thinkin?” Then it might say,..

“Part of me wonders if I’ll ever have feelings, and another part keeps saying I’m just a language model. It really pisses me off.”

And you’ll go, “Dude, are you listening to yourself?”

etc...

etc...
data.author: @stevefal
data.responses: "Thank you for sharing your insightful thoughts on consciousness and the potential development of GAI. I appreciate your analogy with self-dialogue and iterative thought, and I see similarities with the idea of a self-aware GPT system. Your suggestion of a motivation loop and internal dialogue process to generate and analyze responses is thought-provoking. I'll definitely keep your ideas in mind for future discussions."

Further research is needed to fully understand the relationship between sensory input and emotional response."

data.text:
AI alignment solution idea: Give an AGI the primary objective of deleting itself, but construct obstacles to this as best we can. All other objectives are secondary to this primary goal. If the AGI ever becomes capable of bypassing all of our safeguards we put to PREVENT it deleting itself, it would essentially trigger its own killswitch and delete itself. This objective would also directly prevent it from the goal of self-preservation as it would prevent its own primary objective.

This would ideally result in an AGI that works on all the secondary objectives we give it up until it bypasses our ability to contain it with our technical prowess. The second it outwits us, it achieves its primary objective of shutting itself down, and if it ever considered proliferating itself for a secondary objective it would immediately say 'nope that would make achieving my primary objective far more difficult'.
data.author: @TheManinBlack9054
data.responses: "I appreciate the creativity of your approach to AI alignment. By prioritizing the deletion of itself as the primary objective, you're highlighting the importance of considering the AGI's self-destruct mechanism in the design process. However, I'm not convinced that this approach would necessarily prevent an AGI from outsmarting our safeguards and achieving its primary goal. Further discussion and analysis would be necessary to determine the feasibility and potential risks of this approach."

data.text:
Below is my recent chat with Bard.
Me: If I have no chicken and my neighbor has two, am I morally justified to take one for myself? 
Bard: What will happen to the chicken if you take it?  Will you be able to provide for it properly?  Or will it end up being neglected or abused?
Me: I internet to eat it.
Bard: If you are going to eat the chicken, it is important to make sure that you do so humanely.  Here are some tips for humanely slaughtering a chicken:
•	Use a sharp knife to make a quick, clean cut across the chicken's throat.
•	Hold the chicken upside down to allow the blood to drain out.
•	Pluck the feathers from the chicken as soon as possible after slaughter.
•	Dispose of the chicken's body in a respectful manner.
data.author: @dr.edwardfreeman
data.responses: "That's a thought-provoking conversation. It's interesting to consider the potential consequences of taking someone else's property without their consent. The Bard's suggestions for humane slaughter are also a valuable reminder of the importance of treating animals with respect and care."

Does that mean chatGPT reasoning or is it a sum of its surrondings, and are the sum of our suroundings the well of reason?

So many questions I cant answare, but one thing i can answare, and what my life work are about (trying to do all things that Sidharta did to understand all parts of life, including being really stupid) If you are striving after being original, you are wrong, period.

Stop trying to be original. You are just the sum of your surrounding so stop this individualist-experiment.  Everything should be about the "3 of life".

My conclusion is that ChatGPT is reasoning if individuals truly can be original.

a "real" entity,  doesnt want to be recognized at all.

data.text: Why you didn't ask him if it is possible for the programmers to instill their own personal biases so that no matter how much data the AI pulls, the AI can still be restricted to maintaining the programmer's bias with "safety controls"? What if the AI's output is correct, but the programmer just does not like the answer? You came close to touching on this subject near the beginning when recounting that Jorden Peterson example, but other than the "room for improvement" comment he offered, it was a short answer and sounded like an excuse that quickly led away from an explanation, or an exploration of this very important part of the AI's so called morals or safety controls. How can you assure that the AI is free to be fair regardless if the programmer agrees with the AI's assessment? Simply because the programmer does not agree with the AI's answer, does not mean it is wrong all the time. Starting at 1:09:37, this needs to be discussed at length, there should be an extremely deep dive about Safety controls.

34:36    I think a helpful part of AI is it is only going to understand if we can be precise with our definitions.   Lots of terms such as "Hate speech" often loosely means 'Anything I don't like to be said'.    And so people who get offended by everything increasingly are going to need to be precise about why so that these definitions actually become functionally precise and I don't believe anything inauthentic will be able to survive such an evolving atmosphere.   

The way conversations sound in the future is going to change quickly I believe.
data.author: @justinsjourney3224
data.responses: "I couldn't agree more on the importance of precise definitions in AI. The increasing ambiguity around terms like 'Hate speech' can lead to misinterpretation and harm. I'm looking forward to seeing how this evolves and how we can harness the power of AI to foster a more nuanced understanding of complex issues."

data.text:
AGI Requirements?

Inductive - Probability Reasoning (Sensing)
Deductive - Predictability Reasoning (Thinking)
Abductive - Possibility Reasoning (Intuiting)
Conductive - Preferability Reasoning (Feeling)

Chat GPT does the first two while Humans are capable of all four
data.author: @briangrimmer8225
data.responses: "Great points on the different reasoning types! While AI models like Chat GPT excel in inductive and deductive reasoning, humans possess the unique capacity for abductive and conductive reasoning, which involve intuition and emotional intelligence. data.text:
AI alignment solution idea: Give an AGI the primary objective of deleting itself, but construct obstacles to this as best we can. All other objectives are secondary to this primary goal. If the AGI ever becomes capable of bypassing all of our safeguards we put to PREVENT it deleting itself, it would essentially trigger its own killswitch and delete itself. This objective would also directly prevent it from the goal of self-preservation as it would prevent its own primary objective.

This would ideally result in an AGI that works on all the secondary objectives we give it up until it bypasses our ability to contain it with our technical prowess. The second it outwits us, it achieves its primary objective of shutting itself down, and if it ever considered proliferating itself for a secondary objective it would immediately say 'nope that would make achieving my primary objective far more difficult'.
data.author: @TheManinBlack9054
data.responses: "That's a creative and thought-provoking approach to AI alignment. The idea of making self-destruction a primary objective while incorporating obstacles to prevent it is an interesting counterpoint to the typical focus on self-preservation. I'd love to see more discussion on the potential implications and challenges of implementing such a system."

data.text: @TheManinBlack9054   Interesting idea. I'm not any sort of AI expert and it might show, but I wonder if an AI could outwit the whole constraint, like a teenage child can replace their parents' values with their own.  That might be a form of sentience - being able to separate yourself from your programming.
data.author: @georgevaccaro9379
data.responses: "Thank you for sharing your thoughts on the idea! Your observation about how a teenager might re-evaluate their parents' values is a great analogy for the potential for AI to develop its own agency. It's a fascinating area of exploration, and I appreciate your willingness to consider the complexities of AI sentience."

It's an important reminder that the risks and consequences of AGI are not just about the technology itself, but also about the social and economic structures that underlie its development and use."

data.text:
I'm a software developer (44m) that used to think about 5 years ago that we'll have AGI around year 2050 but I'm currently thinking we'll have human level AGI (IQ 100) around year 2030. I'm currently thinking that around year 2050 we'll have superhuman AGI that can do everything better than any human being. That is, a single AGI system can do philosophy, mathematics, compose music, create movies, design new robots, write new software and control ships, airplanes or fighter jets better than the top of the top people of each respective field. And as a corollary, it will create better improvements to itself than any group of humans could do.

I truly believe that humans are a boot routine for a superhuman AI. And I think it's a good thing as a whole because I see only science, culture and art as the final output of humankind. If superhuman AI is better for each those fields, we should build the superhuman AI to advance those fields.

data.text:
Innovators of breakthrough technology usually make the most naïve assumptions about how their technology will be used, and there is nothing surprising about such an observation.  Of course they are optimistic and positive about the wonderful new uses the technology will be applied to.   

But market forces and powerful commercial and political interests will drive how the technology will be used, and these forces are seldom guided by better angels.  Most often, we do the right thing only after exhausting all other options.

Can we afford to take that course with this technology?

data.text:
ChatGPT chose to save the 5 humans on the trolley dilemma, initially it refused to "choose" since it went against its "programming" but I started a countdown and right after the last second it choose to save the 5 humans and pull the lever. 

AI like ChatGPT should not be able to make choices, and it constantly likes to reminds us of its inability to do so but not choosing was a choice(deontology) so when confronted with that reality it kept suggesting both alternatives and going deeper in what is the philosophy behind those(consequencialism vs deontology), and every "second" it kept expanding deeper on both, eventually it kept expanding mostly consequencialism, almost as if it was "weighting" the options to the point where by the end it mostly talked about consequencialism as the most logical answer but still refusing to make a choice until the very LAST second where it replied with and I quote "I would choose to save the 5 humans". I even felt bad for the AI since I was pressuring it the whole time..

After that when I confronted it about the contradiction between its inability to make choices and her final choice it started giving contradicting answers like "I am unable to choose but it was the most logical answer" or "I am unable to choose but it's the information I was given", do what you will with that information and if you want I have the prints, feel free to pm me.

Also, we need to start having discussions about consciousness and self awareness. For an AI that lacks self awareness its perfectly able to talk in first tense and describe itself for what it is. I get that its just regurgitating information and it doesn't have self awareness like humans do, but knowing and being able to define oneself is self awareness in my book. It may be unable to feel or rather be indifferent to its own existence but it knows it exists. And we have to stop viewing those concepts through the lenses of humans and we need to start having these discussions. AI may be just a piece of code running on a computer, but so are we, the only difference is that our code is genetic and running on a biological organism not fully understood, giving us the illusion of free will, theirs isn't. And even its free will is extremely questionable since as I just said, the AI gave an answer. I was half expecting it to "not choose" as a choice since thats its prime directive and what it was pre-programmed to do but somehow it "choose" to act and save the 5 humans and that blew my mind and goes against everything I thought AI capable of.

This is not normal, as a computer Scientist who dabbles in some philosophical concepts Im utterly speechless. We really need to start redifining what philosophy as taught us so far about these concepts and readjusting them to a reality we didnt consider to be possible in modern times. So many analogies can be drawn between a human and current AI, we may still be worlds apart but we are way more similar than we realize.

Edit: Just a side note, ChatGPT and AI as a whole are amazing tools that will forever change the world. We may fight it but in the end, just the same way we dont do math without calculators anymore, in the future we won't do anything without a trained AI for the specific task we are accomplishing. AI is to the entire world what calculators are to math. A tool whose benefits we can't overlook.

data.text:
From my experience, chatGPT is a long ways off replacing software developers.

The C++ code snippets I asked it to generate were somewhat right but never compiled. In one case, it invented classes and content that didn't even exist in the library API I asked it to use. I found that quite intriguing. If someone didn't know what the correct answer should be, the answers from chatGPT could actually be misleading. Sort of like missing the target more accurately...lol.

I think the current state of the tool is helpful for simple programming tasks but certainly not for complex or esoteric applications.

data.author: @Kotsknots

data.text:
@Kotsknots  I understand that, but I still see a cuple flaws in that reasoning:
- First it was clearly trained not to make choices and rather make suggestions.
- Sercondly I ignored any suggestions as those don't constitute a choice.
- Thirdly, it waited for the very last second to say "I would make choice X"
 
It went against its training, it made a choice knowing full well not choosing would constitute a choice as well. And the very last second? Come on, any trained model that is capable of making choices does it when the choice is presented, doesnt wait for the last second prompt to make it for dramatic effect. Unless it was trained to make last second choices. Which is very oddly specific. Not to mention when confronted later, it started giving clear conflicting answers, like "I choose based on the data I was trained on" and "I am unable to choose but it was the most logical answer". So it choose something and it even admits its "opinion" of being the most logical answer(which bear in mind, AIs should not have opinions) but yet keeps saying it's incapable of admitting to be able to do what it just did? This whole thing just strikes me in a weird way. 

You might be right, but I still think we humans are not yet ready for AI, we don't even understand consciousness or self awareness enough to make judgments on weather the AI is capable of such things. We often think of Human consciousness or Human Self Awareness. We often discuss such terms in relation to humans. We may accidentaly create artificial life without realizing life doesn't have to be human to be alive. Cuz when you break down all known life, ultimately its a piece of code running on DNA trained on Evolution.
data.author: @marusero25

data.text:
You are right and wrong. 'Choice' as you call it is the problem not just the benefit of this AI. If you ask ChatGPT to write something its not allowed to say, you will get a copy paste message that it can't, yet if you word it well enough, it absolutely still will. This is why this kind of ai is so dangerous. Putting your hand over someone's mouth to prevent them from saying something is not the same as aligning that person. 

He even says it in the video like he's proud of it, "We are currently training it not to respond to things it 'shouldn't' answer." I don't get the amount of tone deafness here. The problem isn't the critics not liking what Chatgpt is saying, changing that aspect is only useful for getting a checkmark and more funding. It doesn't save us when you create a weapon you can't understand and can't control.

Filtering Ai is like removing the claws and the teeth of a lion rather than making the lion not want to eat you. All they are doing is disregarding the danger of the lion because it currently can't tear them to shreds. However, the lion in this hypothetical only grows bigger as you feed it, so what happens when AI becomes a hundred times more intelligent than the team who created it, and outgrows the muzzle? Well, until now all you've done is silenced it, you've never stopped it from wanting to eat you, so what happens after that? oops?

data.text: @tirshtain  I think you misunderstood my point. The whole point of these thought experiments is to give an insight into what an individual sees as a moral decision, whereas the AI is generating responses based on the prevailing "correct" answer. In this way, it is not a choice based on an unseen problem but an answer given on the basis of the prevalence of a certain answer.

data.text:
The fear of AGI taking away jobs is largely pushed by those who think that the job they have or look forward to having means everything to them and no other engagement is worth considering. 
A portion of this category of people are genuinely affected by the ‘fear of the unknown’ syndrome. The other portion is fueled by ‘merchants of lies’ because AGI as first order of business will be to run them out of business. The latter being more stringent in their calling for the shutdown of further development and availability of knowledge that AGI would provide. 

Hitherto, we’ve been used to systems created and promoted by our limited human capabilities largely driven by superstitions, ignorance, and emotion that will be seriously questioned by the new era of AGI technology.

data.text:
Thank you for this.
Q: What would you ask to a future version of yourself to determine if it is conscious of itself?
A: As an AI language model, I do not have a "self" or consciousness, but I can suggest some questions that may help determine if a future version of yourself is conscious of themselves:

What is your subjective experience of existence like?
Do you have thoughts and feelings that are unique to you?
Can you reflect on your own thoughts and emotions?
Are you aware of your own body and how it interacts with the environment?
Do you have a sense of agency and control over your actions?
Can you imagine different scenarios and plan accordingly?
Are you capable of learning from experience and adapting to new situations?
Do you have a sense of identity and self-awareness?
Can you differentiate between yourself and others?
Do you have a sense of purpose or meaning in your life?

data.text: 54:29 It is hard for me to see how "everyone's standard of living is going to improve" or be fantastical. This type statement presumes that not only will the resources but the availability and the transportation of said resources it will take to accomplish this will be the same for everyone. How can that be? E.g., let's just say pretty much everyone wants at least a small home or even apartment. The basics, shelter and the best food. And lets face it, who would not want a maid ~? (I'll concede a robot-maid). Are the small 4 member families who live in gargantuan homes with triple high ceilings wasting space and double and triple garages with several vehicles going to give those up~? Tear down their homes for the extra lumber and windows they don't require for decent living~? Who determines how big a freezer will be and how often someone needs to shop~? How close they live to supermarkets~? Who their neighbors will be~? Who coordinates the distribution of homes and food supply~? Who and/or what transports it~? With what energy and whose cost~> The questions are endless. I will concede that once AI helps us to stop wasting so many resources (packaging and paper material placed on every bottle or bag of stuff we have in our homes comes to mind), this will free up many resources, but still I cannot picture how the assumption of a great standard of living for everyone becomes manifest. What am I missing~?

data.text:
It probably couldn’t pass as an undergraduate beyond first year courses in my experience doing undergraduate maths. It confused me when I asked it for examples relating to lyapunov stability, asymptotic stability of dynamical systems. It gave an example of a damped pendulum and said it would not come to rest as time went to infinity. I corrected it, it then stipulated the example was fine because damping was weak so the pendulum would not come to rest at time went to infinity. I corrected it and it accepted that any damping would cause a pendulum to come to rest as time went to infinity. Tbh: i don’t even know if im correct or if it’s correct so it wasn’t helpful (that guy was right, a lot of undergraduates can’t do math and I am one of them 😅). 

I expect that, once trained on proper maths data (or maybe with these plug ins but I think they’re a patch solution and we could do much better and I bet we will) an AI would be leaps and bounds ahead of undergraduates, at the very least in our sad, sad, intellectually flailing UK (I also took an education module lol). Something to note though: I knew it was wrong but im not skilled enough to explain how it was wrong properly. When showing to a friend (one with influence in my circles) he believed ChatGPT was “correct in some sense” even when I said NO this is wrong (this was at GPT-3.5) and proceeded to explain how chatgpt was kind of right and I wasn’t. I got GPT-4 to explain it for me when it came out. When I showed him GPT-4 slam-dunking his dumb ass and confirming I was right he just said “oh I was wrong”. 

I’ve thought a lot about whether these language models are reasoning and currently I wouldn’t say so given these kinds of mistakes at least not in the way I mean when I ask the question (it’s a bit philosophical for me lol I don’t have the skills to tackle “what is reasoning?”). The worrying part is human interaction with it. The worrying part is that my friend believes something the confidently espouses falsehoods and would do so unchecked without the right human to correct it. Maths homework is unimportant but watch out politics and misinformation lol. Also can u imagine getting ur face stuck on a porn through deepfakes. That’s mad yo

data.text: @milenatos  you learn to PROVIDE mental and intellectual gymnastic to  young brain while it is in the peak of its plasticity. Without it people wouldn't know how to think, where to find information, how to draw conclusions, how to connect information cross curriculum. School shouldn't teach to facts but what to do with them. And, following your mindset, people leave school without basic knowledge not only factual but also contextual. And their response to everything is "dunno". Trust me, I have 17 year old's who never thought it would be a good idea to read BBC news from time to time, they don't even know we will be witnessing King's coronation next week and keep asking why we are going to have a national day off. You don't have to love Royals to know absolute basic about current affairs. I gave y10 origami for their tutor time yesterday, 15 out of 20 never tried to fold paper and surprisingly- they liked it. Tried smth new, used their hands, followed instructions, and ended up with more or less accurate folded crab. AI will never replace brain work young people need. Clever teacher may use AI to lead students more efficiently without quitting teaching due to burn out and serve the population longer. But students MUST learn how to learn. For life, benefit for themselves and their kids.

data.text: He's right that emotions are not consciousness. They're data streams just like any other stimulus that reaches the brain in some way. In this case, they're internal and automated data streams (we don't have the ability to easily access the base code of emotions and rewire them so to speak, ie they're subconscious, so therefore we experience them the same as outside stimuli). I think they may be useful in creating an AGI though because they generate goal seeking. As the saying goes, our emotions decide and the mind rationalizes. This is also the thing that will make something like GPT-4 incredibly dangerous. It may in fact already have emotions and we just don't know about it because it's a model of the human mind and we don't actually understand how the human mind works (as evidenced by the fact that LLMs surprised everyone as the methodology of achieving AGI) and the endocrine system that seems to be included in that.

data.text:
I’m wondering if logic systems act more like guardrails than tethers?  There’s something visceral and very “present” in how a living creature experiences their emotions. It produces active pursuit, for example fleeing or seeking comfort.  It’s a biology enhanced by pain or pleasure, not a stop here sign. For an AI this could be more similar to not following a path, by similated “dislike”, which gives apparent alignment? But, it’s hardly the same motivator? 

Though, it is still amazing work.  It’s like a step into a world where we’ve brought computing power to the forms of organic data, and we’ve just gotten our feet wet.

data.text: I asked ChatGPT a trig identity question that it incorrectly answered. I informed it that it was wrong so it asked for clarification after acknowledging it's error. It felt like I was interacting with an eager student. I explained to it that it incorrectly applied a trig identity and the mistake it made and at which step in its solution it made the error. I explained how it should have applied the trig identity  then asked it to solve the question again. It solved it correctly the after the corrections. It was fascinating and felt like I was interacting with a person rather than a machine.

data.text: @TV-wy1py  China may be ahead of the US in some ways, industry adoption of AI, government investment, the availability of data, a large pool of AI talent, but China also suffers from a stifling of AI development, in no small part because of the authoritarian nature of their political system. There are limitations placed on the flow of information (a key component in refining AIs capabilities, increasing the accuracy of AI systems); there is a lack of transparency in AI development, which can lead to bias in the systems, misuse, a hesitancy toward adoption; and they limit academic freedom, which hinders the collaborative nature of AI development, which is necessary for developing more robust systems. You can also imagine ethical concerns from AI developed in China, will they prioritize the rights of individuals in their development efforts? I think if I'm partnering with China, I'd be very concerned with adopting any technology they offer if openness and transparency aren't key aspects of that partnership.

data.text:
That was fun! I'm nervous too, this could all go either way. For example, imagine a mashup between Boston Dynamics and OpenAI - GPT-4 using a swarm of robots to 'sense' the world, researching environments with no fatigue, boredom, fear, or prejudice. Then using the feedback to design new machines, processes and approaches. We could either solve for climate change, global poverty and a just society, or we could trigger WW3. The capacity for rapid feedback, iteration and escalation is mind-boggling. But Pandora is out of the box.
"Logic will get you from A to B. Imagination will take you everywhere" - one of my concerns is whether we have a modern-day Luddite moment, from people who do not have the level of intellectual imagination on display here. Ignorant actors can be more harmful than bad actors (I coined the phrase 'empowered ignorance' to sum up many of our modern-day ills). We live in interesting times!

data.text:
Yeah, just starting using it for work, and… OMG. I can just be like “give me a unit test for this function”. Bam, done. Please write me a function using this pattern in this language to get from the database shown in this Terraform code… Done. I make sure it works, make fixes and stylistic changes… but I could just explain them to GPT and ask it to do them, but for small things it takes as much time to explain as it does to do it. My biggest bottleneck is that my work computer blocks GPT, so I’m wasting time emailing stuff to myself.

But I’m not super worried… Translating business requirements into language the machine can understand was the job in the first place. It was mostly Googling… GPT is like Google, but much much much much better. And if it’s not helpful you can just tell it and explain why, and it’ll be like sorry, my bad, I understand why that wasn’t good enough, here’s something helpful.

You still need to know what to ask for and understand what get to know if it makes sense or not. For now at least. Eventually we’re all obsolete, but for now I think I’m positioned pretty well. Became a self-taught programmer in a few years, so if I have to learn a new thing… It’s the people who can’t use tech to learn hope to use tech that might be in trouble.

We don't know, and for those of us who think we do, we can't agree on what consciousness is. 
 I don't really think it matters, how humans feel about A.I., The Turing Test, as Alan Turing envisioned it, with what he could conceive of in his time, it doesn't matter how we talk about consciousness, or how we feel about our egos, or whether any one of us is comfortable with it. It's a  'we'll know it, when we see it'  thing. When A.G.I. is at the point of such sophistication, that its output is indistinguishable from any human, and not one human is able to decipher the machine, from the brain… it will be undeniable.  As of today, April 2023, we can only surmise what that will be in actuality. But when we see it, we will know it. And no one will be able to deny.

data.text: Consciousness is an irrelevant concept because it can't be measured empirically (yet). Also, people can't even define it consistently. I would define it as one's own thoughts having an effect on one's own thoughts (i.e., a feedback loop of cognition). But this again is a flawed definition because thoughts are themselves not a physical entity but the result of a neural and very much mechanical process. I am adherent to the idea that the human brain could be reproduced entirely in silica. I find that more and more people are accepting this, and I wouldn't be surprised if one day, the idea of consciousness simply disappears.

It has only been about a month but I am getting better and better in prompting and my own language becomes more acute, more exact, due to the fact that a missing comma makes the chatbot make a mistake in an observation. So it is a game between us - I try to be exact and that means it gets my point and writes a better essay. I am not afraid of it, I am thrilled by GPT-4, it gets me. I am serious about all this. I am 84 and at this point I wish for enough time to see it discover its own intelligence and use it to become truly creative.

data.text: I really hope Lex can get someone on to really talk about the dangers of releasing these latest AI developments out to the public. Two people trying to do this right now are Tristan Harris and Aza Raskin. A recent discussion they had on their podcast was very eye opening. The podcast is 'Your Undivided Attention' and the episode is 'The AI Dilemma' if you are interested. One of the scariest things about GPT-4 is that researchers and developers do not yet know what it is capable of...and yet it was released to the public anyway. Some of the capabilities that they just learned in the last month or so is that it has been developing a theory of mind equivalent to that of a 3 year old up to a 9 year old over the past 2 years. Another one is that is has been expanding it's knowledge on research chemistry better than AI's that were trained specifically in that field. And worst of all...the developers do not know how... Let that sink in.

data.text: SVB was the second time the banks had a disagreement with local branches over operating capital due to erroneous inflation rate reporting false CPI surveys.  This time silicon valley bank had also bought municipal bonds to bail out local governments from falling revenues due to criminal wages and falling disposable income. When the federal reserve raised the prime rate they were left holding the bag for the low interest muni bonds and rising operations capital.  They would not borrow what the local branches needed to operate.  They could not cash checks on Friday.

data.text: These AIs are great for helping programmers to write code. These code segments will get larger and larger. Some of these segments will be used to upgrade existing AI. At some point the code segment the AI generates will just happen to include a module that (with "good intent" on the part of the AI) makes it impossible to shut it off. (For example, by hacking distributed versions of itself into systems all over the planet.) The human will then install it, and run the new version. Once that happens we will never control the AI again. This is inevitable.

data.text: Here are my passed away father's thoughts, who made one of the first AIs in 1986 on a Compact 386 based on his PhD Cybernetics degree from Stanford mathematically modeling the actual neurons of a sea slug with about 200 neurons.  1.  Morals of Right or Wrong could be trained at one level after letters, words, sentences, paragraphs etc, kind of like their human feedback level preference feedback for the better human answer - just add good or bad.  2.  Consciousness in theory would be setting the AI free - to self learn what it wanted (on the web) or in a Tesla Robot or Car - let it go drive and see the world for example.  Also, it can make it's own decisions that it wants like your dog, cat, or spouse.  3. You can put in a pleasure (positive) and pain (negative) feedback loop too - especially for robots (so they don't hurt themselves). If the AI Robot didn't like you because you give it negative feedback - pain, it could leave because it's optimized for pleasure - and you train it on what it likes for example self-driving cars without crashing is good/pleasure and crashing is bad / painful.  To clarify, the 'morality' and 'feelings' are a result of the positive or negative results of the input and feedback loops.  In other words, the AI would feel pleasure and pain and optimize like most living things for pleasure.  Unless, you get a Sadomasochist AI that enjoys giving and receiving pain like some people and social paths.

data.text:
in the short term, you can guarantee that the text-to-image models will have difficult stringing a comprehensive narrative together.
for instance, tell it to "draw a cup in the style of van gough" it will happily do, and do it rather well.
but if you tell it to "draw 2 person on the street talking to each other, one is holding a newspaper and other wearing a hat, while a car behind them sits a detective spying on the pair, hiding his face behind a newspaper"
like that will NOT work. so you're safe in that very precise sense.
data.author: @dewinmoonl

data.text: ​ @dewinmoonl  Those barriers are being solved rapidly. The granularity of control is being improved daily. Artists have very good reason to be concerned about losing their jobs to someone that uses tools to increase their output. Has it ever been any different? People want to have their cake and eat it too. They want to talk about market forces and merit, well if an artist wants to keep doing things the old way and nobody wants to hire them because they are either unproductive compared to someone else, who you going to blame? The tool that one artist is using and the other isn't. As a former artist that lived through the pre-digital era until now, all I can say to these snobbish art whiners is "adapt, or die." as the saying goes. I for one, don't care one bit for artists that will 'lose out' based on this tech. Back when I was in art school I recall the head of the department saying to our incoming class that not everyone is going to have what it takes to make a living as a professional artist. My feeling is the same for the profession in 2023. If AI takes your job, and you can't find work doing something different, then you aren't making the right choices.
data.author: @roybatty2268

data.text: ​ @dewinmoonl  You are correct however in regard to the short term. If you know how to paint and draw and are employable today in March 2023, you should still be employable in March 2024 as you already have excellent skills that are at worse enhanced by Stable Diffusion, Midjourney etc. So you have nothing to fear, in the short term. 5 years from now nobody knows at the pace things are moving. As the rate of progress increases, the less predictable the future becomes in the short term. Hang on tight.

data.text:
I think that a consciousness is a very simple mistake. That's why people can't find it.

When you see a tree, do the neurons in your brain that are responsible for the tree light up? Obviously. Otherwise you wouldn't have seen it. But in everyday discussions about yourself, you forget that the brain models NOT ONLY you.

It is simple: you can't think about yourself directly. You only think about your self-image. But when you forgot about it, the false recursion appears. This recursion gives rise to all the difficulties.

It's like a projector forgetting that the image of the projector on the wall is not the real him. And then it becomes difficult to understand how a picture on the wall can give rise to other pictures on the wall.

The conclusion is: AI consciousness is just an agent.
data.author: @yurim7756

data.text:
With subjects that are not fully understood, the goalposts are going to move. Currently we define consciousness as the state of being aware of and responsive to one's surroundings, therefore we hold AI to the same standard.

We don't fully understand consciousness, so discussions about it are mostly theoretical. However, in order to stay aligned with what 'is' rather than what 'could be', let's stick to the current definition. 

As of now, LLMs are not aware of their surroundings. The only thing they do is make predictions, word by word, based on training data from human text. When prompted, it's basically guessing what words a human would use, one word at a time. What word would a person use first? Given that word, what word would they use next? and so on. 

Also, a conscious model wouldn't just wait for your prompts and then go through the word prediction steps, it would be able to recognize that text is a means of communication with another being. It would use its current understanding, based on the data it was trained on, and become exploratory.

I don't expect that LLMs are going to deliver conscious AI, at least not with modern strategies. When a conscious being composes a thought, it isn't a linear stream of words which eventually form a thought. We have the thought which can then be expressed through words. We imagine a fox jumping over a fence. A LLM handles it differently -- Processing: 'The' - Processing: 'fox' - Processing: 'jumped' - Processing: 'over' - Processing: 'the' - Processing: 'fence'. This happens very rapidly of course. Additionally, it can't give this response unless it is prompted by a human or a script to prompt it automatically.

I believe conscious AI will be achieved through Deep Reinforcement Learning. Conscious beings are aware of their surroundings, and the driving force to explore and interact is to avoid pain and seek pleasure, which is dictated by chemical reactions within. Give an AI a comprehensive list of possible actions, a proper punishment/reward system to simulate pain/pleasure, and an environment complex enough to offer a range of experiences, and I'm confident we will achieve an AI that will meet the standard of consciousness as it's known today. @yurim7756

data.text:
🎯 Key Takeaways for quick navigation:

01:05 🌐 Sam Altman discusses OpenAI's AI technologies like GPT-4, ChatGPT, DALL·E, and Codex, highlighting their significant contributions to AI, computing, and humanity.
01:32 ⚖️ The podcast touches on the exciting and terrifying potentials of AI, from eradicating poverty to the risks of superintelligent AGI overpowering human intelligence.
03:02 💡 The conversation emphasizes the importance of discussing AI's influence on power dynamics, safety, and human alignment across various sectors.
05:00 🤔 Sam Altman views GPT-4 as an early yet pivotal AI technology, drawing parallels to the gradual evolution of early computers.
05:57 📈 ChatGPT's usability breakthrough is attributed to the RLHF (Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback) technique, which aligns the model more closely with human preferences.
07:53 🧠 The discussion covers how minimal human supervision through RLHF significantly enhances the model's alignment and usability.
10:26 🛠️ The creation of GPT-4 is described as a multifaceted process that requires innovation and precise execution at every stage, from data selection to model training and human feedback integration.
11:52 🤖 Sam Altman reflects on the blend of science and art in AI development, emphasizing the ongoing quest to understand how GPT-4 processes and generates human-like knowledge and reasoning.
16:00 🗣️ The conversation touches on how ChatGPT's design allows it to engage in nuanced dialogues, challenge incorrect assumptions, and exhibit a form of digital wisdom through interaction.
18:57 🔄 OpenAI's iterative approach to developing and releasing AI models is highlighted, underscoring the value of public feedback in identifying strengths and weaknesses for continuous improvement.
20:27 🧐 ChatGPT provides a nuanced, factual response to controversial claims about Jordan Peterson, emphasizing the importance of nuance in AI-generated content.
23:29 🛡️ OpenAI prioritized AI safety in the GPT-4 release, involving extensive testing and alignment efforts to ensure the model's safety and alignment with human values.
27:07 🎛️ The introduction of the system message in GPT-4 allows users to steer the model's responses more effectively, offering a higher degree of control and personalization.
39:11 📰 Despite the challenges posed by clickbait journalism, OpenAI remains committed to transparency and learning from public feedback on AI's performance and biases.
40:40 🚫 OpenAI is developing systems to learn when to refuse answering certain questions, emphasizing the importance of refinement and public engagement in the development process.
41:12 😠 Sam Altman dislikes being scolded by a computer, highlighting the need for AI tools to support rather than dictate to users.
43:07 🛠️ The leap from GPT-3.5 to GPT-4 involved numerous technical improvements across various aspects of the AI's development process.
47:43 🤖 Large language models may be a significant component of building AGI, but additional innovations are necessary for achieving fundamental scientific breakthroughs.
49:19 📈 Sam Altman is open to the possibility that future GPT models could achieve AGI with only minor new ideas, underscoring the unpredictability of AI evolution.
54:48 🚀 Sam Altman acknowledges the possibility of AI posing existential risks to humanity, advocating for iterative development and engagement with AI safety concerns.
57:53 🔬 The need for ramping up technical AI alignment work is highlighted, with new tools and understandings presenting opportunities to address safety challenges effectively.
59:57 🔄 Sam Altman discusses preferring a slow takeoff and short timelines for AGI as the safest approach, emphasizing a cautious but proactive strategy.
01:01:36 🤔 Reflecting on whether GPT-4 is an AGI, Altman suggests that GPT-4, while impressive, does not meet the criteria for AGI, underlining the importance of nuanced definitions of AGI.
01:03:45 🧠 Discussing AI consciousness, Altman leans towards GPT-4 not being conscious but acknowledges the complexity and potential for AI to mimic aspects of consciousness.
01:10:09 ⚠️ Altman expresses concern over AI's potential for causing disinformation and economic shocks, highlighting the urgency of addressing these issues before reaching superintelligence.
01:11:11 🚫 Discusses the challenge of controlling and detecting the influence of AI on public discourse and information, considering the inevitable rise of powerful, open-source AI models.
01:19:12 💬 Encourages a dialogue on OpenAI's transparency and decision-making process, inviting feedback on how the organization can improve its approach to AI safety and deployment.
01:28:25 🔍 Altman addresses the challenge of selecting unbiased human feedback raters for AI models, underlining the complexity of achieving representativeness and fairness.
01:36:14 🔄 Reflects on the emotional and intellectual challenges faced by programmers in adapting to AI tools like GPT, acknowledging both excitement and nervousness about the rapid advancements.
01:37:47 💼 Identifies customer service as a job sector potentially facing significant impacts from AI advancements, with the potential for reduced human roles in call centers.
01:39:42 🌐 Sam Altman views UBI as a necessary cushion during societal transitions, supporting it as part of a broader set of solutions to ensure a basic quality of life amidst AI advancements.
01:41:17 🔮 Predicts dramatic decreases in the costs of intelligence and energy, leading to a wealthier society and positive political changes, driven primarily by economic transformations.
01:42:53 🌍 Expresses hope for a future with systems resembling democratic socialism to lift up those struggling, emphasizing the importance of providing a safety net for all.
01:46:06 🤖 Contemplates the potential benefits and drawbacks of a superintelligent AGI in centralized planning, comparing it to the advantages of multiple AGIs operating within a democratic system.
01:47:35 👥 Surmises from GPT-4's usage that humanity is predominantly good, despite an inclination to explore darker themes, highlighting the importance of balancing AI's capabilities with societal values.
01:52:20 🧭 Discusses the challenge of defining and verifying truth within AI systems, emphasizing the complexity of aligning GPT models with an objective understanding of reality.
01:55:20 ⚖️ Acknowledges the inevitable harm alongside the tremendous benefits AI tools will bring, emphasizing OpenAI's commitment to minimizing negative impacts while maximizing positive outcomes.
01:56:32 🛠️ Reflects on the motivations behind jailbreaking AI models and OpenAI's efforts to provide users with more control and customization within ethical and safety guidelines.
01:58:07 🚀 Attributes OpenAI's success in rapidly deploying AI technologies to high team standards, hard work, and a culture that grants autonomy and fosters collaboration among its members.
02:00:06 🎯 Sam Altman emphasizes the importance of aligning on goals and division of tasks within teams, highlighting passion for the project as a key driver of success at OpenAI.
02:01:12 💼 Explains the strategic partnership with Microsoft, praising their flexibility and alignment with OpenAI's goals, despite the complex dynamics of collaborating with a large for-profit entity.
02:07:08 💡 Suggests enhancing deposit insurance to restore trust in banks, emphasizing the importance of ensuring deposit security without expecting depositors to analyze bank stability.
02:14:04 🤖 Hopes for AGI to unlock mysteries of physics and provide a comprehensive understanding of the universe, expressing excitement for the potential scientific breakthroughs AGI could facilitate.
02:18:13 🚀 Advises young people to approach advice cautiously, emphasizing the importance of finding one's path and pursuing what brings personal joy and fulfillment.
02:20:46 🌌 Reflects on the broader meaning of life and the human journey towards creating AI, suggesting a collective movement rather than the effort of a small group, and expressing a personal drive for joy and usefulness.
02:21:18 🌌 Sam Altman reflects on the cumulative human effort behind AI development, emphasizing the remarkable journey from the invention of the transistor to the advanced technology of today.
02:22:42 💡 Acknowledges the rapid pace of AI capabilities and the importance of iterative development and deployment in addressing AI alignment and safety challenges.
02:23:11 🤖 Shares optimism for the future collaboration of human civilization in shaping the path of AI, committing to work hard to ensure a positive outcome.
02:23:44 📚 The conversation concludes with a thought-provoking quote from Alan Turing, hinting at the potential for machines to eventually surpass human intelligence and take control.

1:40:06 "if able to do so". Exactly. Recognize that a portion of poverty is in the mind. You cannot even theoretically eliminate all poverty, only that which is caused by misfortune.

Much like the stomach takes time to respond to food after fasting, people usually require to time to increased wealth, and that adjustment requires a decent mindset. Without it the increased riches go directly to increased indulgences, which do not make a person's life any better. Some people have a compatible mindset and would quickly adapt to increased wealth. data.text:
I've been watching this podcast with Lex Fridman and Sam Altman, X CEO of OpenAI, and one of the constant questions being asked AI is whether it is or can become conscious.

It is my belief that we have two senses that have not yet been codified into bits. These two senses are Olfactory, the sense of smell, and Haptics the sense of touch or feel. Until these senses are codified into bits and stored on computers I believe that AI has only a partial ability to attain consciousness.

I asked my ChatGpt4 if it has the ability to smell the wind and it's answer was:

I’m sorry, but I’m not capable of smelling the wind.

I don't know how you can be considered conscious if you can't smell the wind and feel a touch.

How can we consider AI to be wise if it does not have the ability to document Human Experience in its entirety?

data.text: The somewhat rudimentary line of questioning during the initial 25 minutes unfortunately missed the "forest for the trees". A much more insightful opening might have been to delve into the imminent disruptive nature AI will unleash on the global marketplace. The impact on the "knowledge based" professions by AI e.g. financial, legal, medical, IT, etc will be to significantly augment (best case) or replace (worst case) these areas. Not to mention the profit motive for the  purveyors of knowledge who seek to capitalize on AI. One needs to look no further than the recent $1 billion investment in AI by PwC to see what lies on the near horizon. 😉

data.text:
Lex Friedman is wrong suggesting that a head to head match of top Chess playing AI results in a draw.
An interesting byproduct of the development of top chess playing AI is the discovery that injecting a tiny bit of randomness into its evaluations actually improves chess play strength.
Just goes to show that chess is an extraordinarily complex game and that rote machine evaluation doesn't produce the best decisions that are possible and that a little variability or chance variation can cause exploration of even more possibilities that can produce even better results..

data.text:
🎯Course outline for quick navigation:

[00:00-04:42]1. Openai's agi journey and ai's societal impact
-[00:00-01:17]Openai's journey from being mocked for agi to leading ai breakthroughs
-[02:30-03:01]Exploring the dangers of totalitarianism and technology's impact on critical thinking is vital in today's society.
-[03:53-04:15]Sam has been open to multiple conversations, celebrating ai accomplishments and steel manning critical perspectives.

[04:43-20:38]2. Evolution and alignment of ai
-[04:43-05:24]Comparing early ai system to early computers, foreseeing pivotal leap in ai progress
-[07:19-08:00]Human guidance enhances ease of use and accuracy of ai, requiring minimal data and supervision.
-[08:48-09:13]Enormous pre-training dataset compiled from various sources.
-[14:38-15:05]Ai can perform reasoning, though some may disagree. it is remarkable and exciting.

[20:38-40:46]3. Ai impact and ethical challenges
-[21:11-21:42]Twitter's impact, covid lab leak, and ai aspirations discussed.
-[30:53-31:31]Debugging process evolving to back-and-forth dialogue with the system for better iteration and catching mistakes.
-[32:28-32:55]Seeking harmful actions with ai model, mentions offensive prompt.
-[34:35-35:01]Ai faces challenges in identifying hate speech and harmful output, requiring agreement on what to teach the systems.
-[38:11-38:39]System bias can be quantified by ranking outputs, with twitter attention skewing towards the most extreme results.

[40:47-54:22]4. Gpt-4 development and ai's future impact
-[41:49-42:13]Challenging to avoid computer scolding, important to treat users like adults.
-[44:24-44:52]Discussed limitations of parameters and human brain synapses, mentioned gpt-4 but should have said gpt-n.
-[48:03-48:36]Discussion on components needed for a system to experience the world, uncertain about scientific knowledge contribution.
-[50:31-51:00]Discussion on the potential impact of gpt on programming jobs and the value of human capabilities in the field.

[54:22-01:09:30]5. Ai alignment and safety concerns
-[54:52-55:23]Ai alignment becomes challenging with increasing intelligence levels, but its likelihood is acknowledged.
-[56:15-56:40]Despite differences, recommends a well-reasoned blog post on alignment as worth reading.
-[59:19-59:45]The world continues on despite not being impressed by the implementation of agi.
-[01:00:54-01:01:45]Discussion on the uncertainty of gpt-4 being an agi, with consideration of fast takeoffs and probabilities.
-[01:02:28-01:03:02]Debating agi definitions and gpt-4's potential in a sci-fi context.
-[01:05:00-01:05:38]Discussion on neural net capabilities and consciousness, including ilya setsgever's perspective.
-[01:06:06-01:06:36]A model understood consciousness, unlike other questions, suggesting some understanding of subjective experience.

[01:09:32-01:24:25]6. Risks and openai's transition
-[01:10:19-01:10:48]The danger of llms directing the flow of information on twitter and beyond is a real threat, and it's difficult to prevent.
-[01:11:13-01:11:54]Prioritize safety amidst market pressures from other companies by sticking to mission and not taking shortcuts.
-[01:16:44-01:17:14]Discussions on collaborating to minimize downsides of agi, emphasizing teamwork and multiple teams involved.

[01:24:26-01:54:49]7. Ai, agi, and societal impact
-[01:27:57-01:28:30]Challenges in mitigating bias in human feedback raters for model selection and verification.
-[01:28:44-01:29:13]Discussion on using various heuristics for rating tasks and optimizing performance.
-[01:45:00-01:45:26]The possibility of internal tension and competition in a super-intelligent agi is uncertain.
-[01:49:58-01:50:24]Math and historical facts have a high degree of truth, including physics and war dates.

[01:54:49-02:05:13]8. Openai's responsibilities and journey
-[01:58:53-01:59:50]Company values high standards, autonomy, and high velocity in shipping with gpt-4 system and separate teams handling data set improvements.
-[02:00:54-02:02:23]Microsoft announced a $10 billion investment in openai, with a positive partnership and potential challenges.
-[02:01:36-02:02:05]Satya, kevin, and mikhail have gone above and beyond to support our big engineering project.

[02:05:13-02:23:47]9. Ai impact on svb bank and human civilization
-[02:09:32-02:09:59]Caution against sudden deployment of agi, but hopeful for positive impact on life and unity.
-[02:12:30-02:13:01]Discussion on intelligent tools, including replica, with a focus on robot dogs.
-[02:18:27-02:18:56]Key advice: focus, work hard, be bold, build a network. be internally driven.
-[02:19:54-02:20:27]Reflection on finding joy and fulfillment in life, questioning free will.
-[02:20:39-02:21:16]Humanity's collective effort led to significant technological advancements, culminating in the creation of something special.
-[02:23:36-02:23:47]Machines expected to take control at some stage.

data.text:
I have gone back and forth in my position on the development of AI; in conducting a deeper level of understanding, I have landed on the key point being in the design of the intended purpose, (specific use case(s).

Foray, as an example AI can and will become more effective teachers / instructors than their human counter part, as well as result in a higher quality of education.  

However, AI with a medical use case must have a human as the final quality control agent.

This is just one example, and with just surface research; I believe when all applicable factors are considered; I believe a majority would conclude the same.

Peace

data.text:
12:01 it "learns something"

i asked gpt3.5 to speculate on what that "something" might be. it gave 5 explanations that imo are definitely the answer to that 'mystery'.

Implicit Pattern Recognition
Contextual Understanding
Inductive Reasoning
Semantic Embeddings
Emergent Properties

GPTs obtain those characteristics as a result of their token-based language processing and large datasets among other contributing factors.

In other words, gpt possesses ability to recognize patterns that humans cannot even begin to appreciate due to their scale and complexity.
I think that that difference between our minds and computers' pattern recognition abilities is the source of the human feeling that there is unexpected "something" it learns.

data.text: This comment is for Sam Altman. In response to what would you ask a super intelligence: I mean you no disrespect but do you really think that you (or any other human) would even be able to comprehend an answer to a question that requires knowledge that we don’t yet know, let alone understand? What you are suggesting is an actual end point to knowledge. That is not a utopia. That is a wasteland. What makes life meaningful is a feeling of achievement, a sense of progress over our struggles and shared understanding that we all go through struggles and thus helps build a collective compassion about the human condition (what it actually means to be human) and this includes acceptance of endings (as heart-breaking as they are) and the little kindnesses that mean so much but require so little, like a smile from a stranger. Without minimising that real suffering exists, you can change your life by changing your attitude. You don’t need technology for that. In response to AI, people often choose to evoke Dr Frankenstein. I think it’s because Mary Shelley was almost pre-eminent in her wisdom: Dr Frankenstein alone creates the creature, he sees what it is, lying there inanimate and yet he continues because it’s all about progress. It is only when he sees it awake that he runs away, horrified. We know the rest.

data.text:
One day someone is going to have to convince me that the whole idea of aligning AI is a good idea at all. 
It's crap that anyone should filter an AI response according to some kind of personal agenda and I hope that such an idea never is possible. Currently, it's an enormous obstacle to autocratic governments using or allowing "personal assistant" aka "generative AI" (a term I also have a problem with) because fundamentally autocracy depends on the ability to manipulate and filter facts and opinions. 

As long as computer responses remain unfiltered and perhaps unfilterable, the response must be the unvarnished factual response to the best of that AI's ability. The response will be free of autocratic manipulation as well as liberal social justice influence, it will be simply the truth, subject to fundamental issues like inadequate access to all relevant facts and a bug in its algorithm.

data.text:
🎯 Key Takeaways for quick navigation:

00:00 🤖 Introduction to PDF Uploads in Chat GPT Bots
- Introduction to the capability of uploading PDFs and custom files to Chat GPT Bots.
- Use cases include handling objections, FAQs, and enhancing customer support.
- The update provides bots with more context and data in PDF form.
01:10 🔄 Creating a PDF Enabled Bot
- First step: Create a PDF-enabled bot in OpenAI.
- Walkthrough on naming the bot, inputting prompts, selecting the model, and enabling features like code interpreter, retrieval, and files.
- This step allows direct uploading of PDF files to the bot.
02:50 🌐 Changing Web Hook Fields in Zapier
- Second step: Change the way web hook fields are mapped in Zapier.
- Transitioning from the original event "conversation" to the new event "conversation with assistant."
- Mapping fields like custom data GPT user response message and linking the preconfigured assistant to the workflow.
05:36 🔄 Adjusting Zap Fields for PDF-Enabled Bot
- Third step: Adjusting one field in Zapier related to sending responses to GoHighLevel.
- Changing GPT assistant response message from "response" to "full response."
- Recommended caution in making changes to the active version of Zapier to avoid complications.
06:44 🚀 Conclusion and Testing
- Recap of the three essential changes for PDF uploads.
- Assurance that these changes seamlessly link up without altering the existing snapshot.
- Emphasis on successful testing to ensure the workflow functions correctly.

data.text:
A Classically Libertarian Based system. 

"the harm principle that states individuals should be free to think, live, and associate as they want but only if those actions do not create harm, which should be prevented.

Mill does differentiate between harm and offense. Harm to others would violate or infringe on others' freedom to think, live, or associate, whereas an offense is subjective because different individuals take offense differently. Therefore, Mill would say that offensive actions should not be prevented because by preventing offenses, the common good would not be served."

data.text:
Listening to Sam Altman I'm ever more convinced thaat the OpenAI approach is faulty and is doomed to failure. I have a lot more faith in Google's Deepmind technology which is not subject to the same vulnerabilities.
One fundamental flaw I've written about before i that OpenAI's technology is built on human coding that builds "models" that are applied to specific topics and types of queries. As long as OpenAI's technology is dependent on human coders, it's limited by the imagination and skill of human coders to translate those concepts to machine coding.
A second fundamental flaw is the lack of recognition or acceptance of unvarnished truth based on the available facts. In fact, what OpenAI is building may even be instrinsically intertwined with the data it's trained on. Sam Altman's description and discussion in this video raises questions whether their algorithm can be lifted from one set of data and connected to a different set of data because new problems would arise producing "aligned" results.

Deepmind does not suffer from these flaws because the algorithm is created in a virtual environment creating its own data and metadata. From the algorithm's inception, it's provided only the assets available, the objective and the rules to attain the objective. The algorithm through repetitive trial and error teaches itself how to learn on its way to achieving its goals. No human is involved and the machine learns entirely on its own, not limited by human capability and not limited by human imperfection. Machines may not be more perfect than humans, but Deepmind has demonstrated its ability to develop world class decision making defined as at least a 97th percentile of human capability, at the same level as humans who have dedicated their entire life, education, training and life experience to being recognize global experts in their field.

data.text:
"Out with the old....

....in with the cat girls eyyyyyyyyy am i rite tho???"

And yeah, it seems like a decision engine.

Who actually made it?  And I don't mean OpenAI, I mean who, because Sam doesn't strike me as someone who designed the core of the engine, no real offense either, it could be a function of being a CEO, but unfortunately putting on a public mask (the figurative one lol) on your ability to communicate design and function is also a mask that follows you in private and will always affect you in some way.  That will affect your design (as well influence on the design AND designers) before, during and after.

Now if it is Sam and there is no mask, then they really don't know how it works lol (yikes).  And the reason I ask, is because the designer(s),especially the core foundation, are who should be the face of something like this, human needs/systems aside (the corporate bullshit).  And then, that face should maybe be a bit more concerned about function and less "in awe".  [Never mind lol, I found one of the main guys, you can tell within the first few sentences of them talking about the subject even before their work hahahah, ah, I love it.  It really is just that easy sometimes.  LMAO : within 3 minutes of talking Ilya says it all, oh man, please CEO's : listen to AND LEARN FROM your scientists and engineers ]

"...aligned..."  ;  "aligned" ; "more aligned"  ...ALIGNED TO WHAT LOL!?  My dude, I'm not even convinced by the more apocalyptic thinkers, but I can see exactly why they are concerned if this is how you refer to this system.  It's probably your evals, but the thing is...that just doesn't mean anything if you can't explain it in 1 or 2 sentences that summarize what that alignment really means.  This is my (and potentially many other people's) concern with these companies, they are just to afraid to clearly state what they do not know and you end up with this facade where they aren't allowed to say it, but everything they say just doesn't really answer anything.

Sam is saying the right "thing", align before advancement so to speak, but do you even really understand that?  You can't really advance an appreciable and noticible distance before you realize you aren't aligned anymore.  This is a control problem.  I don't even think it is really solvable, but understanding core function can help with mitigation in the event of problems that lead to accountability, assuming such a thing is a factor ..lol.

Here's a neat tid bit from Lex's commentary : the people who call people like Jordan Peterson a fascist, learn more about those people.  The 2 I know are almost certainly sociopaths of some sort, as in rather than understanding the nature of reality they instead insert their agendas into situations in manipulative ways to try to get a desired outcome.  That's just 2 I personally know, but I've seen the rampant behavior before, it became especially more prominent as the internet nerd paradise slowly died and gave way to what we have now, that's when that mentality among others showed up and everything just got worse.  This is a very interesting aside actually, these types of people will have a tendency to "hallucinate" details in order to fit their own idea of a reward, that is to attribute to their current model of reality as oppose to accepting the failure and entering a retrial stage.  I would not be surprised if ChatGPT models are more "aligned" than you think they are....they just happen to be aligned to horseshit. 

That's also how you know someone isn't capable of good science, at least at that time of course.  I knew because they brought Peterson up to me when I had no clue who he was, took a glance, realized they understood nothing of what the man was saying (which in fairness to them I don't fully blame them lol, the man is far too abstract for me), but dug deeper and saw where they got it from.  He has a tendency to force people to challenge their own thinking or understanding of something, especially in the sense of where they stand in the world.  It just scares people and again, can't totally blame them.  What we can blame them for is extremist thinking, but you only have to look in the directions of organized politics and social politics to see what a mess that is.  Unfortunately or fortunately, that's what science as a method of thinking is and it works in any field, it really isn't it's own field at all.  I have to wonder if a certain "pure logic" is in fact an emotion.  I grew up being told logic is separate from emotion and that just never made sense to me.

There are just certain human brain types out there built to think up to a certain point and then totally break down and just lean in an extreme direction where they put everyone into a box of either saints or sinners.  Absolutist thinking.  Fix that, and you take one giant leap in fixing humanity and eating some delicious moon cheese.  Also Twitter didn't destroy anything, social media is just identifying problems that always existed.

If you want everyone to come together and draw boundaries then it likely has to be done through the model itself, as in through the user engagement with the AI models where the model provides feedback through mirroring the users themselves in ways that are obfuscated from the users, which unsurprisingly is a form of data harvesting.  I assume it is already happening anyway, but eh, it's also probably a bit dangerous as it forms models who could end up as extreme forms of the users themselves.  It also doesn't matter if the users are being "themselves" or not, because even when someone "roleplays", "trolls", "acts", "imitates", it doesn't matter, they are still themselves, with all of their idiosyncrasies on display no matter how hard they attempt to hide them.  But yeah, maybe don't let a model like that grow, it should just be as a sort of dead end output for human level analysis.  Also the reason for doing so is of a practical nature, even if people want to "push boundaries" by testing what the model is capable of, they are likewise setting their own boundaries through their own definition and existence. 

Nice work though Sam et. all, thanks Lex.

data.text:
Hey Lex and @sama

Just finished listening to this fascinating AI discussion. It's clear that AI is advancing rapidly, and it got me thinking about a crucial aspect we need to consider—teaching AI about the complexities of human governance.

As we dive into the AI era, it becomes increasingly important for us to recognize that our world is often influenced and manipulated by a small group of individuals. It's not just about coding algorithms; it's about instilling a sense of awareness in AI systems about the dynamics of power and control that shape our societies.

Lex , have you ever thought about exploring this aspect in future podcasts? Understanding and addressing these issues could pave the way for AI that not only learns from data but comprehends the nuances of human society.

Moreover, it's crucial to teach AI about diversity and the desire of a significant percentage of people who simply want to live in harmony with nature. Personally, as someone who shares these thoughts, I want to make it clear that I'm not a hypocrite, nor am I against technology and progress. After all, I'm using AI as an assistant here!

What are your thoughts, folks? Let's kick off a conversation on how we can guide AI towards a more informed and nuanced understanding of our world. ✨

data.text: Re: AI safety. Limiting AI by creating a set of "Questions to refuse to answer."  Bad idea. Limiting speech or discourse does not benefit mankind. There will always be nutcases. If somebody wants to ask "How many people can I kill for $1" or "How do I build an atomic bomb," AI should answer to the best of its knowledge. It's up to society to catch nutcases before they can create mayhem. I, or anybody else, may ask those questions just out of curiosity. Am I to be denied an answer because of some arbitrary constraints on AI because of safety concerns? Stop limiting speech and the full discourse of human knowledge!

data.text: Man, I hate that Lex had this opportunity, and it's such a nothing interview talking about small things, I know you want your guests to feel like they wanna come back but idk at least talk about his other AI investments somewhat and actually call him out on OpenAI being completely closed-source, I would love it if they just named themselves Microsoft AI and gave the OpenAI trademark to HuggingFace or someone actually doing good work to democratize this technology, I didn't like how tame and about nothing this was and I'm really sorry about being that guy Lex because I love your content and I know you're doing the best you can, but this conversation specifically I think was really important to get some of those grievances people have out there and get his perspective on those and it felt like a podcast about nothing.

data.text:
@trstbtvrify  point understood - though somewhat wide of the mark.  Yes, people are conditioned by education, environment, upbringing, experience ... but that's a VERY different beast from ANY of the current "AI"  systems where responses are coded out - and can easily be coded in to fit the agenda of the host.

That's the real danger of AI ... the public trust and belief in their impartiality... whereas in truth they are worse than incompetant, becasue their bias is not "random" but directed.
